ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic dumpling class (FOODON_03315873). No specific pork-and-chive boiled dumpling entry exists; closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers filled wheat-based dumplings; no pork-and-chive-specific code exists. closest_parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for pork and chive boiled dumpling. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of pork and chive dumplings is approximately 240 g (roughly 8–10 pieces), providing about 444 kcal, 20.4 g protein, 19.7 g fat, and 46.8 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — Dumplings, pork and vegetable, boiled
nutrient_source.organization: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) — Foodo / Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database used as primary regional source for boiled pork-and-vegetable dumplings. Values for micronutrients not directly listed in HPB were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, item 'Pork dumplings, steamed/boiled') and the input enriched_json values retained where consistent with both sources. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork dumpling profile (approx. 2.8 g per 100 g). Cholesterol estimated at 35 mg per 100 g based on USDA SR Legacy pork dumpling data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Traditional Chinese Foods
gi_source.organization: Asia Pacific Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 55 for boiled wheat-flour dumplings (shui jiao) sourced directly from this peer-reviewed publication. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (240 g × 19.5 g/100 g = 46.8 g) ÷ 100 = 25.74, rounded to 26.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (320 mg per 100 g; ~768 mg per standard 240 g serving) from seasoned pork filling and soy sauce, with additional sodium risk from dipping sauces
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 4–5 dumplings per meal, omit or minimise the dipping sauce, and request low-sodium soy sauce in the filling preparation to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 55) from refined wheat wrappers, partially attenuated by co-ingested protein and fat from pork filling and dietary fibre from chives
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may include pork and chive dumplings in a balanced meal plan by limiting intake to 6–8 pieces (approximately 150–200 g) per sitting, pairing with non-starchy vegetables, and monitoring postprandial blood glucose response given the significant refined-carbohydrate load from the wheat wrappers.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.8 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (320 mg per 100 g) from pork filling and seasoning; cholesterol approximately 35 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease, pork and chive dumplings should be consumed in moderation (no more than one standard serving per occasion), prepared with lean pork mince to reduce saturated fat, and served without high-sodium dipping sauces to support blood pressure and lipid management goals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork and Chive Dumplings (猪肉韭菜水饺, Zhūròu jiǔcài shuǐjiǎo) are a quintessential Northern Chinese boiled dumpling (shui jiao) featuring a filling of seasoned minced pork and fresh Chinese chives (jiu cai) encased in a thick, hand-rolled wheat-flour wrapper. The dough is characteristically chewier and more substantial than Southern-style wonton wrappers, yielding a satisfying al-dente bite. Chinese chives contribute a pungent, garlicky, and mildly sweet flavour that complements the savoury, slightly fatty pork. The dumplings are boiled in water until the wrappers turn translucent and the filling is fully cooked, then served immediately with a dipping sauce of black vinegar, soy sauce, and chili oil. Culturally, they are a symbol of prosperity and family reunion, traditionally prepared and eaten during the Lunar New Year, though enjoyed year-round as a main meal across Hebei, Shandong, and Beijing. Per 100 g, they provide approximately 185 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 8.2 g fat, and 19.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is notable at 320 mg per 100 g, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic index is 55 (low-medium boundary), moderated by the protein and fat content of the filling. Chinese chives provide antioxidants and modest dietary fibre (1.2 g per 100 g). A standard serving of approximately 240 g (8–10 pieces) delivers around 444 kcal and 768 mg sodium, which should be factored into daily dietary planning.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, sugar) are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo data for boiled pork-and-vegetable dumplings and corroborated by USDA SR Legacy pork dumpling entries. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and cholesterol were estimated from USDA SR Legacy data as HPB does not publish full fatty acid profiles for this item. Micronutrient values (vitamins, minerals) are drawn from the input enriched_json and are plausible against USDA SR Legacy comparators but are not directly verified against a single named HPB record, warranting medium rather than high confidence. GI value of 55 is directly cited from a peer-reviewed Asia Pacific Journal of Clinical Nutrition publication.
notes: 1. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific boiled dumpling preparation is not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy at the level of precision required; value left as -1 per numeric hygiene rules. 2. Saturated fat (2.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.2 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4 g), and cholesterol (35 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork dumplings (boiled/steamed) as HPB does not publish full fatty acid profiles for this dish; adaptation documented in nutrient_source. 3. trans_fat_g set to 0.0 as no added hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy comparator. 4. The input portions array contained a duplicate entry (two identical '1 serving (240g)' records); only one 240 g serving is used. 5. The input GI notes described GI 55 as 'medium GI' but the Asia Pacific Journal of Clinical Nutrition source and standard GI classification (≤55 = low) support categorisation as low/low-medium boundary; gi_category set to 'low' per standard classification. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 55 × (240 g × 0.195 available carbohydrate) ÷ 100 = 55 × 46.8 ÷ 100 = 25.74, rounded to 26.0 g. 7. This is a Northern Chinese dish; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable and left null. G6 multilingual compliance is satisfied as the cuisine is Northern Chinese (not Singaporean), requiring only Chinese-script coverage, which is present in both simplified and traditional forms.
